items.evidence_text:
Client 1: Mine is about $102,400 gross.
Client 2: And mine is $74,900 gross from work.
Advisor: One sec—let me note that. So $102,400 plus $74,900… that’s $177,300 gross household income. Does that sound right to you both?
Client 2: Yeah—okay, that’s the number. I was rounding down in my head.
Client 1: Right, $177,300. That’s what I had on the last tax summary.

Advisor: Perfect. Let’s switch to spending. About what do your monthly expenses run—mortgage or rent, utilities, food, travel, all-in?
Client 2: We’re around $5,500 a month.
Client 1: It’s a bit higher—closer to $5,800 monthly.
Advisor: Okay, so $5,800 a month total expenses. Elizabeth, does that include everything—like insurance and the random stuff—or is that just the core bills?
Client 2: That’s basically everything. I mean, some months are higher, but $5,800 is the right average.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Elizabeth—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Hi Alex. So, I’m 62, and I want a clear path to retire around 68 without guessing.
Client 2: And I’m Elizabeth—58—and I’m thinking more like 70, but I want to know we’re okay if one of us has to stop earlier.
Advisor: Got it. And just to confirm household basics—this is the two of you, two adults in the household, right?
Client 2: Yes, two adults.

Client 2: Yes, joint.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, investable assets total—so not counting the house—do you think you’re around $600k-ish?
Client 1: Yeah, about $607,000 total investable.
Client 2: That sounds right.
Advisor: Okay. Quick check—any insurance policies we should factor in? Life insurance, long-term care, anything through work or privately?

Client 1: And the house—
Advisor: Yep, let’s grab that too. Primary residence value, roughly?
Client 1: About $388,450.
Advisor: Great. And just to be clear, the home is jointly owned, right?
Client 2: Yes, joint.

Advisor: Great. And do you also keep cash at the bank separately—checking/savings—roughly how much?
Client 2: Yes, bank savings and checking—about $62,550.
Advisor: Okay. Any other investments—private markets, alternatives, anything like that?
Client 2: Yes, there’s a private markets thing on an advisor platform. That’s about $97,400.
Client 1: And the house—

Client 2: No, none. It’s just us.
Client 1: Yeah, zero dependents.
Advisor: Okay, zero—got it. And you two are married, correct?
Client 2: Yes, married.
Client 1: Married, yep.

Advisor: When you say “other,” you mean not one of the big common ones—so I’ll mark it as “Other” for state, unless you want to name it.
Client 1: Yeah, “Other” is fine.
Advisor: Perfect. Now, risk—this is always a little fuzzy. Earlier you both sounded like you want growth but you don’t want to feel exposed right before retirement. Would you call your risk tolerance conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 1: Moderate.
Client 2: I’d say conservative—

Client 1: Two adults, yeah.
Advisor: Okay—two adults. No one else.
Advisor: Now, a slightly weird one: for our planning assumptions, we tag the “market” we’re operating in. For you, it’s the U.S. RIA channel—basically a U.S. registered investment advisor setup. Does “US RIA” sound right for how you think about this relationship?
Client 2: Wait, are you asking if we’re in Rhode Island? Because—no.
Advisor: Oh—good catch, Elizabeth. Not Rhode Island. RIA as in Registered Investment Advisor.

Advisor: Yeah, totally—spring can’t decide what it’s doing. Okay, going back to that earlier point about taxes and keeping things simple near retirement, I want to confirm one tax item.
Advisor: David, Elizabeth—what bracket are you usually thinking you’re in right now? Like the federal marginal bracket.
Client 1: We’re in the 35% bracket.
Client 2: I mean—yeah, 35%. Not our effective rate, but the bracket.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you for clarifying that—marginal versus effective trips people up all the time.

Advisor: Okay, so we’re in that ballpark. David, do you remember your gross pay, and then Elizabeth yours? Doesn’t have to be perfect.
Client 1: Mine is about $102,400 gross.
Client 2: And mine is $74,900 gross from work.
Advisor: One sec—let me note that. So $102,400 plus $74,900… that’s $177,300 gross household income. Does that sound right to you both?
Client 2: Yeah—okay, that’s the number. I was rounding down in my head.

Client 2: April 30th… 1968.
Client 1: Right—4/30/68.
Advisor: Okay, April 30, 1968. Thank you.
Advisor: One more quick labeling thing—just so I’m consistent when I refer back to notes. David, you’re Client 1, and Elizabeth, you’re Client 2. Is that how you want me to label you?
Client 2: Yes, I’m Client 2.

Advisor: Got it—healthcare as the occupation group.

Advisor: Before we do, I want to make sure I’ve got your work situation right. Elizabeth, you’re still working currently, yes?
Client 2: Yes, I’m employed—still working.

Client 2: Yep, spouse/partner.
Client 1: I mean—yeah, that’s right.

Advisor: Good question—income versus balance. Here I mean the income you receive in a year—interest and dividends paid out—not the account value. Does the $148,400 refer to the income number, David?
